name: Shelter Construction (Basic)
tagline: Windbreaks, lean-tos, and simple enclosed spaces that stabilise temperature and protect people, fire, and stores
problem:
Humans tolerate a narrow comfort band without clothing and fire support. Wind strips convective heat; rain destroys insulation; sun causes dehydration; predators exploit sleeping vulnerability. The basic shelter problem is creating a bounded microclimate: reducing wind speed, shedding precipitation, reflecting radiant heat from fire, and separating occupants from damp ground.

This is not aesthetics — it is thermodynamics and hygiene. Raised sleeping platforms reduce parasitic insect contact; overhangs keep fuel dry; smoke holes balance ventilation versus heat retention. In cold climates, small enclosed volumes around a fire are dramatically easier to heat than open camps.

Shelter also protects technology: cordage rots faster when constantly wet; clay dries unevenly in gusts; charcoal kilns need wind shields. A civilisation rebuilding needs the very first week to include reliable nightly shelter, not only because of comfort but because cognitive work (tool repair, planning) requires dry hands and stable light.

In space settlement, shelter is an absolute pressure and radiation barrier. On planetary surfaces, the 'basic' shelter involves managing regolith overburden to block solar and cosmic radiation — a direct analog to the primitive earth-berm or cave, but with different mass-per-area requirements (typically 3-5 meters of soil).
overview:
Basic shelters combine structural members, covering, and anchoring. Structural members are poles harvested green (flexible) or dry (stable), lashed into tripods, A-frames, or ridge-pole configurations. Coverings include thatch, bark sheets, hides, woven mats, and snow blocks (igloos) in polar contexts. Anchoring uses stakes, rocks, and tensioned cordage in guy lines.

Site selection dominates performance: avoid 'widow-makers' (dead branches above), drainage channels, and natural wind tunnels. Position openings 90-180 degrees away from prevailing storms. In forests, standing dead timber (conifers) provides straight, dry ridge poles; willow and hazel provide flexible ribs for domed structures.

Lashing is the joining technology: the Clove Hitch starts and ends most wraps; Square Lashings resist shear in crossing poles; Diagonal Lashings pull mismatched angles together. Friction wraps must be seated under tension — natural cordage shrinks/stretches with humidity, requiring a daily 'tension walk'.

Thermal upgrades include reflective fire walls (stones, clay), partial burrows, and insulating layers of dry leaves (duff) or grass between inner and outer skins. A wicking barrier (rocks or dry bark) between the ground and bedding is mandatory to prevent conductive hypothermia.

principles.name: Wind pressure and permeability
principles.explanation: Wind loads scale with velocity squared; openings and loose thatch create pumping leaks. Sealing the lowest windward edge often matters more than perfect ridge sealing.

principles.name: Stack effect ventilation
principles.explanation: Warm smoke rises; a high vent draws air low — without a low inlet you get backdraft and smoke pooling at sleeping height.

principles.name: Ground conduction
principles.explanation: Earth is a large heat sink; bedding platforms or 'dead air' layers (30cm of dry grass) interrupt conductive loss even if air temperature seems acceptable.

principles.name: Material creep under load
principles.explanation: Green wood shrinks as it dries; lashings loosen. Retension after first nights is mandatory maintenance, not failure.

principles.name: Radiation attenuation (Space)
principles.explanation: In space, shelter mass (regolith) is used to slow down GCR (Galactic Cosmic Rays) and SPE (Solar Particle Events). 300g/cm² of material is the 'safe' field standard.

components.name: Ridge pole or lintel
components.function: Spans opening, supports roof loads, defines shelter height
components.madeFrom: Straight trunk (Pine, Spruce, Cedar) or lashed pair
components.criticalNote: Check for heart rot — sound wood rings audibly when struck with a stone

components.name: Rafters / ribs
components.function: Transfer roof covering loads to walls or ground
components.position: Sloped from ridge to ground or wall plate
components.criticalNote: Snow load regions need closer spacing (30cm) and steeper pitch (>45°)

components.name: Thatch, bark, or hides
components.position: Outer skin, layered shingle-style from bottom to top
components.madeFrom: Reed (Phragmites), Birch bark, or tanned skins
components.criticalNote: Thatch must be at least 15cm thick to be truly waterproof through surface tension

components.name: Cordage lashings
components.function: Joins structural members elastically, absorbing wind shock better than rigid nails
components.madeFrom: See rope-and-cordage; root-fibres (Spruce root) are excellent for heavy timber
components.criticalNote: Always include 'frapping' turns (wraps between the poles) to tighten the lashing

components.name: Sleeping platform / wicking barrier
components.function: Insulates from ground, improves drainage under sleeper
components.position: Rear of shelter, raised 10-20cm off the floor
components.madeFrom: Poles with cross-lashed lattice, topped with 30cm of compressed dry leaves

rawMaterials.name: Structural timber
earthLocations: Temperate/Boreal forests (Softwoods for length/straightness; Hardwoods for stakes/impact)
rawMaterials.spaceAlternatives: Extruded aluminium or inflatable polymer bladders; regolith-sintered bricks for permanent walls
rawMaterials.processingRequired: De-barking prevents beetle infestation; charring stake tips in fire improves rot resistance

rawMaterials.name: Thatch and duff
earthLocations: Wetland reeds, tall grasses, evergreen boughs (spruce/fir), or broadleaf litter
rawMaterials.spaceAlternatives: Multi-layer insulation (MLI) blankets or thick regolith berms
rawMaterials.processingRequired: Harvest dry; bundle 'hand-sized' units for consistent thatch courses

rawMaterials.name: Binding materials
earthLocations: Vines (Clematis), inner bark (Willow, Basswood), or Spruce roots
rawMaterials.processingRequired: Soak root/bark bindings in water before use to increase flexibility

buildSteps.title: Site audit and hazard check
buildSteps.description: Look UP for dead branches (widow-makers). Look DOWN for game trails and ant nests. Look AROUND for drainage patterns. Clear a 4x4m area of sharp stones and roots. Level the sleeping area by moving mineral soil.
buildSteps.warningNote: Building in a dry wash (arroyo) risks flash-flood burial even if no rain is falling locally

buildSteps.title: Erect the tripod or A-frame
buildSteps.description: Lash three poles together using a tripod lashing. Raise and spread the legs. Seat the ridge pole into the 'V' formed by the tripod. Ensure the tripod is on the windward side to lean into the breeze.
buildSteps.warningNote: Unsecured poles can slide; dig shallow 'cups' for the leg bases

buildSteps.title: Lattice and thatch (The Shingle Method)
buildSteps.description: Lash horizontal purlins every 50cm. Begin at the ground: tie bundles of thatch to the lowest purlin. Place the next row so it overlaps the first by 50%. This ensures water 'stairs' down the outside.
buildSteps.warningNote: Thin thatch (under 10cm) will leak in heavy rain; test with a bucket of water if possible

buildSteps.title: Firewall and ventilation
buildSteps.description: Build a 1m high stone or clay wall 1m from the entrance. This reflects heat into the shelter while blocking direct wind. Leave a 30cm hole at the peak for smoke.
buildSteps.warningNote: Maintain a 'two-fist' gap between your head and the smoke layer while sleeping

buildSteps.title: Thermal banking (Earth Berming)
buildSteps.description: In cold or high-wind environments, pile loose soil or leaves against the windward and side walls. This creates an air-tight thermal mass. In space, this is the 'Regolith Berm' phase.
buildSteps.warningNote: Berming adds massive weight; ensure structural rafter support is sufficient to prevent collapse

history.event: Postholes and ephemeral structures suggest deliberate windbreaks and small huts at some sites, though preservation is sparse.
Mammoth bone houses and more complex dwellings appear in cold-steppe contexts.
The 3D-printing of regolith structures marks the transition of basic shelter to extra-terrestrial contexts.

impact:
Shelter converts climate into something humans can budget. It multiplies the effectiveness of fire and clothing, enables food storage trials, and supports infant survival — demographic growth tracks partly with housing quality.

Engineering descendants include everything from cabin carpentry to insulated panels and HVAC. The Codex treats basic shelter as the first building code: site, structure, skin, vent.

videos.title: Primitive shelter construction
videos.description: Watch for correct lashing techniques and thatch layering; adapt to your specific biomass and tools.

warnings: Carbon monoxide poisoning from enclosed fire
